text: This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and all other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The ANP Project is comprised of about 475 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the achievement of nuclear propulsion of aircraft.
The ART is to be a power plant system that will include a 60-Mw circulating-fuel reflector-moderated reactor and adequate means for heat disposal.

The fission gas holdup system for the loop is being tested with flow rates of 0.15 and 0.03 scfh of helium with about $0.13\%$ krypton.
After 15 days of operation, no significant amounts of krypton were getting through the liquid-nitrogen-cooled charcoal-adsorption traps.
Operation of 16 loops was terminated during the quarter.

Operation of six such loops was terminated this quarter at the conclusion of periods of either 500 or 1000 hr with maximum sodium temperatures of up to $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Previous evidence that $\mathsf{UF}_3$ was more stable at elevated temperatures than free energy estimates had indicated was substantiated by vapor pressure measurements on $\mathsf{UF}_3$ in the temperature range 1270 to $1390^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

The disproportionation pressure curve that was obtained shows that the disproportionation of $\mathsf{UF}_3$ is far from complete under the conditions prevailing in the vapor pressure cell and that $\mathsf{UF}_3$ is thermodynamically stable with respect to pure $\mathsf{UF}_4$ and uranium metal at temperatures below $1400^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

Mass transfer of large amounts of nickel metal was found in the Inconel forced-circulation loop that circulated molten sodium at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Layers of dendritic metal crystals up to 26 mils thick were found to have formed in 1000 hr.
The use of type 316 stainless steel in the cold portions was found to reduce the mass transfer slightly, but further study of the variables in the process is needed to confirm this finding.

Inconel thermal-convection loops in which $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ -base fuels containing about 2 wt % uranium as $U^{3+}$ were circulated did not show so large a reduction in attack as was found in the loops that circulated alkali fluoride fuels also containing 2 wt % uranium as $U^{3+}$ .
The effect of the hot-leg temperature (1200 to $1600^{\circ}F$ ) on mass transfer was investigated in several Inconel thermal-convection loops operated for 1500 hr.

A definite increase in depth of attack with an increase in hot-leg temperature was noted that may be attributed to mass transfer, inasmuch as loops operated previously for 500 hr did not show the effect of temperature on depth of attack.
The mass transfer effect is masked by the effect of impurities and nonequilibrium conditions during the first 500 hr.

To prove at least that the reactor can be safely scrambled after such a pump failure, it is planned to include cutting off the commercial power (or diesel) at design point $(60\mathrm{Mw})$ as a part of the test procedure.
This will constitute a class 1 emergency, as defined above.
Therefore a scram (class 1 or class 2 emergency) will abruptly insert the rod only enough to reduce $\Delta k / k$ by $1\%$ .
This will be followed by automatic insertion at the rate of $1\% \Delta k / k$ in 30 sec.
The operator may also choose "slow" rod movement of $1\% \Delta k / k$ in 5 min.
Below 10-Mw power operation, the operator will be automatically limited to the slower rate.
The $1\%$ scram will automatically cut off one-half the blowers to reduce the load.
The load will then be further reduced by automatic closing of the radiator shutter in response to a $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ low limit signal for the NaK-to-air radiator outlet temperature.
This will be done with the system isothermal at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and all NaK and sodium pumps operating.
After the checkout of the system, part of the barren carrier will be removed and replaced with fuel-enriched material to provide $80\%$ of the calculated critical amount of $\mathsf{U}^{235}$ .
A flux servo will then be available for holding the reactor at very low power levels (under 10 kw) during low-power experimentation.
The minimum power level for control by the temperature coefficient is expected to be about 300 kw - the estimated heat removal by the radiators with the heat barrier doors open and no air flow.

To take the reactor to the 300-kw level, it will manually be put on not less than a 20-sec positive period, and the barrier doors will be opened when the flux level reaches a set value equivalent to something less than 300 kw.
After stabilization at 300 kw, the control rod will be slowly adjusted to bring the mean fuel temperature to $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (it will have gone somewhat above this).
To increase the power above $300\mathrm{kw}$ , the blowers will be turned on and the shutters opened as desired.

A full-scale model of the ART fuel pump design designated MF-2 was built, and water performance data were obtained for several impeller designs and pump suction conditions for a given discharge-volute design.
The discharge volute and the test impellers were fabricated from brass, and a bearing housing used for the model MF-1 tests<sup>4</sup> was adapted for use in the rotary assembly.
The unit was driven by a direct-coupled, 15-hp, variable-speed, d-c motor.
The pump was installed in a test loop built of 6-in. pipe with head and flow measuring instrumentation and a throttling valve.
A box containing a flat plate 1 in. below and parallel to the pump-suction flange was used.
For the second test, the flat plate was removed and an 8-in. pipe was connected directly to the pump suction.
Since pump performance was about the same in both tests, the remainder of the experiments were conducted with the 8-in. suction line.
A series of ten experiments has been performed, to date, and the test conditions and results are given in Table 2.4.
It is estimated that the efficiency of the pump, exclusive of seal and bearing losses, is approximately $70\%$ at the design point.

Data obtained by varying the running clearance between the lower impeller shroud and the volute indicated that the clearance could be in excess of 0.040 in, without loss in performance at design point.
At low-flow high-head conditions, there was approximately a $10\%$ loss in head because of recirculation in the pump.

At flows of over 400 gpm and speeds in excess of 2000 rpm, a slight noise was detected in the pump, and the intensity of this disturbance increased with increased flow or speed above the threshold values.
The performance of the pump was not altered by varying the suction pressure over a range of -5 to +15 psig; however, the intensity of the noise could be suppressed by increasing the system pressure.
The suction conditions were altered by changing the radius of the inlet eye from $\frac{1}{2}$ to 1 in., and the impeller nut was redesigned to give better fluid guidance.

Operation at high temperature of a critical assembly mockup of the ART is scheduled for late summer, 1955.
The experiment will be performed at zero nuclear power and at about $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The assembly is to include a beryllium island and a beryllium reflector, essentially 12 in. thick, which will be in a helium or argon atmosphere and will contain no sodium.
The system will be filled initially for cleaning and testing with a 50-50 mole $\%$ mixture of NaF and $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .
Increments of $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{UF}_6$ (with the uranium enriched with $93\% \mathsf{U}^{235}$ ) will subsequently be added to the NaF-ZrF_4 mixture in the sump tank.
A single rod will be located within a 1.50-in.-1D Inconel thimble along the vertical axis of the beryllium island.
It will extend from the top of the reactor tank to 10 in. below the equatorial plane of the core.
Annuli of two widths, $\frac{1}{8}$ and $\frac{1}{4}$ in., will be provided for comparison.

The system is being designed to operate isothermally at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ normally, with provision made for short periods of operation at temperatures up to $1300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to enable reactivity temperature coefficient measurements to be made.
Vapor pressure measurements were made on UF in the temperature range 1270 to $1390^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .

While phase equilibrium data show that quite low melting points are available in this system and that adequate melting points are available at $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ concentrations as low as 21 mole %, no composition with physical properties better than those available in the NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ system has been demonstrated.
The Binary System LiF-ZrF<sub>4</sub>

A re-examination of this system by petrographic and x-ray diffraction studies of quenched and slowly cooled samples and by differential thermal analysis was started during the past quarter because of current interest in the NaF-LiF-ZrF $^4$ system.
21 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ was confirmed by quenching experiments on both sides of this composition.
The eutectic temperature found by quenching is about $600^{\circ}C$ instead of the $590^{\circ}C$ indicated by thermal analysis.
The melting point of $\mathsf{Li}_3\mathsf{ZrF}_7$ $(645^{\circ}C$ according to thermal analysis) could not be checked by quenching because of too rapid crystallization.
Decomposition of $\mathsf{Li}_3\mathsf{ZrF}_7$ into $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ and LiF occurs somewhere below $480^{\circ}C$ .
This decomposition explains a series of cooling breaks at about $465^{\circ}C$ and the apparent discrepancy between thermal analysis data and the results of solid-phase studies (reported earlier).
The melting point of $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ $(585^{\circ}C$ according to recent thermal analysis data) was found to be approximately $590^{\circ}C$ by quenching.
The only other binary compound in the system, as yet unidentified, was found by examination of both quenched and slowly cooled samples containing more than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

A careful study of samples of compositions with 57.1, 60, 62.5, and 66.7 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ which were held both above and below the solidus temperature for about 12 hr before quenching indicates that the unidentified compound is probably $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{Zr}_3\mathsf{F}_{14}$ (60%) or $\mathsf{Li}_3\mathsf{Zr}_4\mathsf{F}_{19}$ (57.1%) and that it melts incongruity to $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ and liquid at about $515^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ .
Work on questionable aspects of the compositions with more than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ is continuing.
Manual control of a Variac transformer was found to give a reasonably uniform rate of heating, about $90^{\circ}C / hr$
and of cooling, about $75^{\circ}\mathrm{C/hr}$ .

The base line obtained on the automatic plot of sample temperature vs differential temperature (difference between sample and $\mathrm{Al}_{2}\mathrm{O}_{3}$ reference amplified by a d-c amplifier) seemed to be less erratic when the transformer was controlled manually than when the automatic temperature controller was used.
The compositions studied to date cover the range from 31.9 to 66.7 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

These studies are incomplete, but the data obtained indicate a melting point of $493 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the eutectic at about 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ and a value of $520 \pm 5^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the incongruent melting point of the unidentified compound containing more than 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

The thermal effect at $462^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was apparent on the heating curves of all compositions, presumably because a small amount of LiF present in the $\mathsf{Li}_{2}\mathsf{ZrF}_{6}$ combined with $\mathsf{Li}_{2}\mathsf{ZrF}_{6}$ to give $\mathsf{Li}_{3}\mathsf{ZrF}_{7}$ .
This effect was noted only upon cooling of the mixture with 31.9 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_{4'}$ in which it would be expected that $\mathsf{Li}_{3}\mathsf{ZrF}_{7}$ would be present.

The existence of only one thermal effect, at $565^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , for this composition, in addition to the $462^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solidus transition, appears to verify the existence of the $\mathsf{Li}_{3}\mathsf{ZrF}_{7} - \mathsf{Li}_{2}\mathsf{ZrF}_{6}$ eutectic near this composition.
The Ternary System NaF-LiF-ZrF<sub>4</sub>

Fuel mixtures based on the NaF-ZrF $_4$ system have suitable melting points only if the composition lies close to the compound Na $_7$ Zr $_6$ F $_{31}$ , which is characterized on the phase diagram by a flat maximum at $520^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ eutectics on either side.
The typical NaF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ fuel mixture therefore contains about 45 mole $\%$ ZrF $_4$ .
In the LiF-ZrF $_4$ system, the low-melting region occurs at $50\%$ ZrF $_4$ and $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and therefore there is little promise of improved physical properties here.

The substitution of LiF for NaF was expected to result in lower melting points, because $\mathsf{Li}_3\mathsf{ZrF}_7$ (mp, $645^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ ) melts at a much lower temperature than does $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{ZrF}_7$ (mp, $850^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ ), and $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ (mp, $585^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ ) melts at a much lower temperature than does $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ (inconcurrent, liquidus at $700^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ ).
The liquidus contours were obtained from cooling curves on melts of at least $500\mathrm{g}$ contained in 4-in. graphite liners which were enclosed in nickel pots in a 5-in. pot furnace.

Beginning at the low $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ concentrations, the join between LiF and $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{ZrF}_{7}$ was found to be a quasi binary with a simple eutectic at $625^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 38 mole $\%$ LiF.

The compatibility triangle formed with this join and NaF as an apex contains a ternary eutectic at NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ (51-38-11 mole%) which melts at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and contains NaF, LiF, and $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{ZrF}_{7}$ .
The next quasi binary is the $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7} \cdot \mathrm{Li}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ join.
However, the limit of miscibility of $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ in $\mathrm{Li}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ mixtures at $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ is clearly defined.

This is due to the existence of a lower limit of stability of $\mathrm{Li}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ at $490^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , coupled with the lowering of the limit of stability by increasing amounts of solid solution with $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ and the large thermal effect associated with the decomposition.

At the composition NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ (26-49-25 mole %) the lower limit of stability of the $\mathrm{Li}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ -rich solid solution has decreased to $350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the saturation limit has been reached.

The x-ray diffraction lines for the $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ -rich solid solutions are puzzling in that they show the expected shifts up to about $30\%$ LiF and unexpected, decreased shifts at higher LiF concentrations.
The eutectic occurs at $50\%$ and $590^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The compatibility triangle lying between the $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{ZrF}_7$ -LiF join and the $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{ZrF}_7$ -Li3ZrF7join is characterized by three two-phase crystallization paths with a relatively small temperature gradient; the paths join to form a eutectic at NaF-LiF-ZrF4 (22-55-23 mole %) that melts at $575^{\circ}C$

Superficially, the join between $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ and $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ appears to be a quasi binary, since only the end members are readily identifiable in solidified melts lying on this join, and a eutectic-type break in the cooling curves is found at $445^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ and $31\%$ LiF.
However, $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ melts incongruity at $630^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , and on the $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{ZrF}_6$ limb
of the eutectic, $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ -rich solid solutions are the primary phase.

The eutectic type of halt at $445^{\circ}C$ is observable only on the $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ limb, and it corresponds to the appearance of an $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ -rich phase, which must subsequently react with liquid to give the $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ found as a final product.
The true eutectic between $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ appears to occur at about $435^{\circ}C$ .
These complications have prevented the establishment of meaningful compatibility triangles between 25 and 33.3 mole $\%$ ZrF4.
In this region at $455^{\circ}C$ and the composition NaF-LiF-ZrF4 (34-35-31 mole $\%$ ), there is an intersection of the two-phase crystallization paths involving LiZrF6
The join between $\mathrm{Na}_{7}\mathrm{Zr}_{6}\mathrm{F}_{31}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ is a quasi binary with a simple eutectic at $28\%$ LiF that melts at $455^{\circ}C$ .
Between this join and the $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}\cdot \mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ join there is a eutectic region of peculiar behavior which has not been explained.

The system behaves as though a metastable ternary eutectic between $\mathrm{Na}_{7}\mathrm{Zr}_{6}\mathrm{F}_{31}$ , $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ , and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ forms at $425^{\circ}C$ , but, before complete solidification can occur, a binary eutectic between $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{11}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ makes its appearance.
The melting point of the stable eutectic is $435^{\circ}C$ ; the composition is $\mathrm{NaF - LiF - ZrF_{4}}$ (38-25-37 mole%).
The metastable eutectic at $425^{\circ}C$ is more strongly evidenced at a slightly higher LiF concentration.

Attempts to define the crystallization path between $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{ZrF}_{7}$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ have been rather sketchy and unfruitful; cooling curves contributed relatively little toward the definition.
Similarly, essentially nothing is known about the primary phase field of $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{2}\mathrm{F}_{11}$ .
The region above $45\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ is of little interest from the standpoint of fuel composition, and has been investigated only sparingly.

There was no evidence of melting points below $450^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ when the join between $\mathrm{Na}_{7}\mathrm{Zr}_{6}\mathrm{F}_{31}$ and $\mathrm{LiF - ZrF}_{4}$ (50-50 mole %) was investigated.

The composition of the $\mathrm{LiF - ZrF}_{4}$ incongruent compound containing about $60\%$ $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ has not been established, and attempts to find a quasi-binary join between this compound and $\mathrm{Na}_{7}\mathrm{Zr}_{6}\mathrm{F}_{31}$ or between $\mathrm{Na}_{3}\mathrm{Zr}_{4}\mathrm{F}_{19}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ were unsuccessful.

The quaternary system NaF-LiF-ZrF4-UF4 has been studied as a quasi-ternary system by considering only compositions containing 4 mole % UF4 and by considering the UF4 as a substitution for 4 mole % ZrF4.

The quasi binary between LiF and $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})\mathsf{F}_7$ dropped $10^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , to $615^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , as a result of the lowering of the melting point of $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{ZrF}_7$ by including enough uranium to give an overall composition of 4 mole $\%$ U.
On the NaF side of this join, the quasi-ternary eutectic was found at $590^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ , and the composition was shifted to NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (53-35-8-4 mole $\%$ ).
The lowest melting composition along this join started freezing at $545^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and had the composition NaF-LiF-ZrF-UF (20-55-21-4 mole %).

The composition NaF-LiF-ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (32-35-29-4 mole %) melts at $445^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and resembles a eutectic containing $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})\mathsf{F}_7$ and $\mathsf{Li}_3\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})\mathsf{F}_7$ solid solutions and $\mathsf{Na}_7[\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})]_6\mathsf{F}_{31}$ .
Most of the uranium is in the $\mathsf{Na}_7[\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})]_6\mathsf{F}_{31}$ phase.

It is interesting to note that the melt corresponds stoichiometrically to an approximately 50:50 mixture of $\mathsf{Na}_2\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})\mathsf{F}_6$ and $\mathsf{Li}_2\mathsf{Zr}(\mathsf{U})\mathsf{F}_6$ .
The quasi binary between $\mathrm{Na}_{7}\mathrm{Zr}_{6}\mathrm{F}_{31}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ was not changed much by the inclusion of 4 mole $\%$ UF $_{4}$ .
The eutectic rose only about $2^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (to $457^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ), with an almost imperceptible shift in the direction of more $\mathrm{Li}_{2}\mathrm{ZrF}_{6}$ .
The composition of this quasi-binary eutectic is $\mathrm{NaF}-\mathrm{LiF}-\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}-\mathrm{UF}_{4}$ (31-28-37-4 mole $\%$ ).
Since this eutectic appears to be a saddle point, a quasi-ternary eutectic might be expected at somewhat lower $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}$ concentrations.
The important compositions established in the phase studies described above are summarized in Table 4.1.
A considerable amount of thermal data has been obtained by means of cooling curves with mixtures in the NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ system containing 10 to 50 mole % BeF $_2$ .

The LiF-Li $_2$ BeF $_4$ -Na $_2$ BeF $_4$ -NaF section of the system has been investigated most thoroughly because it is expected that melts containing less than 33 mole % BeF $_2$ will have low viscosity, high heat capacity, and other favorable physical properties.

The thermal analysis investigation of the system has been hampered by undercooling, most troublesome with melts containing more than 33 mole % BeF $_2$ , and by difficulty in obtaining reproducible data.

The low concentration of uranium dissolved in the $\mathrm{NaF - BeF_2}$ (57-43 mole %) mixture at $600^{\circ}C,$ which is in line with results reported by Mound Laboratory, suggests that a large part of the tetravalent uranium present in $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ melts to which $\mathsf{UF}_3$ and uranium metal were added was due to disproportionation rather than to oxidation by impurities in the melt.

The $\mathrm{NaF - BeF_2}$ (77-23 mole % and 75-25 mole %) mixtures and also the ternary mixture NaF-LiF-BeF $_2$ (56-16-28 mole %) shown in Table 4.2 were not purified, and it is quite possible that a part of the tetravalent uranium present was due to oxidizing impurities.

The analyses of material filtered through nickel filters after equilibrating $20\mathrm{g}$ of a purified alkali fluoride- $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ mixture with $4\mathrm{g}$ of uranium metal for $2\mathrm{hr}$ are shown in Table 4.3.

The data in Table 4.3 are insufficient to determine whether the dissolved uranium was due to oxidation of uranium metal by $\mathsf{BeF}_2$ or by oxidizing impurities in the melts, and further experimentation is planned to clarify this point.
